statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
There is a hidden sequence $ a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n $ of $ n $ integers ( $ n \ge 2 $ ). It is guaranteed that $ |a_i| \le 1000 $ for all $ 1 \le i \le n $ .

Let's define a function $ f(x) $ as follows:

$$$
f(x)=\sum_{i=1}^n a_i \cdot |i-x|
$$$

Given $ n $ values $ f(1),f(2),\ldots,f(n) $ , please determine the values of $ a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n $ .

It is guaranteed that the values $ a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n $ can be determined uniquely.

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ). The description of the test cases follows.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer $ n $ ( $ \color{red}{2} \le n \le 300\,000 $ ).

The second line of each test case contains $ n $ integers $ f(1),f(2),\ldots,f(n) $ ( $ -10^{14} \le f(i) \le 10^{14} $ ).

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ n $ over all test cases does not exceed $ 300\,000 $ .

Output Format
For each test case, output $ n $ integers $ a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n $ on a separate line ( $ |a_i| \le 1000 $ ).

It is guaranteed that the values $ a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n $ can be determined uniquely.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
4
4
17 9 9 13
6
-37 -32 -15 4 27 42
5
-26 -32 -24 -4 2
2
420 -69
Output
1 4 2 3
3 6 1 2 -4 -7
-6 7 6 -7 -6
-69 420

Note
In the first test case, the hidden sequence is $ a=[1,4,2,3] $ .

The values $ f(1),f(2),\ldots,f(n) $ are as follows:

- $ f(1) = 1 \cdot |1-1| + 4 \cdot |2-1| + 2 \cdot |3-1| + 3 \cdot |4-1| = 0+4+4+9 = 17 $ ;
- $ f(2) = 1 \cdot |1-2| + 4 \cdot |2-2| + 2 \cdot |3-2| + 3 \cdot |4-2| = 1+0+2+6 = 9 $ ;
- $ f(3) = 1 \cdot |1-3| + 4 \cdot |2-3| + 2 \cdot |3-3| + 3 \cdot |4-3| = 2+4+0+3 = 9 $ ;
- $ f(4) = 1 \cdot |1-4| + 4 \cdot |2-4| + 2 \cdot |3-4| + 3 \cdot |4-4| = 3+8+2+0 = 13 $ .

solutions.title: Editorial
solutions.content:
2195D - Absolute Cinema
Let's plot the graph for $$$y=f(x)=\sum_{i=1}^n a_i \cdot |i-x|$$$ and look at the slopes. How is the $$$a_i \cdot |i-x|$$$ term different from all of the other terms?
In the function $$$f(x)$$$, the only term that changes slope at $$$x=i$$$ is the $$$a_i \cdot |i-x|$$$ term. In other words, the slope difference near $$$x=i$$$ is related to $$$a_i$$$. Let's do some math with these slopes.
The slope left to $$$x=i$$$ is $$$f(i)-f(i-1)$$$;
The slope right to $$$x=i$$$ is $$$f(i+1)-f(i)$$$;
We know that the slope changed by $$$2a_i$$$ near $$$x=i$$$;
Therefore, $$$a_i = \frac{(f(i+1)-f(i))-(f(i)-f(i-1))}{2} = \frac{f(i+1)+f(i-1)-2f(i)}{2}$$$ when $$$1  \lt  i  \lt  n$$$.
Now we know $$$a_2,a_3,\ldots,a_{n-1}$$$. How about $$$a_1$$$ and $$$a_n$$$? Let's define a new function as follows.
$$$$$$g(x)=a_1 \cdot |1-x| + a_n \cdot |n-x|$$$$$$
One value of $$$g(x)$$$ can be found in $$$\mathcal{O}(n)$$$ time by subtracting $$$n-2$$$ terms from $$$f(x)$$$.
Now note the following:
$$$g(1) = (n-1) \cdot a_n$$$;
$$$g(n) = (n-1) \cdot a_1$$$.
Therefore, $$$a_1$$$ and $$$a_n$$$ can be found from $$$g(1)$$$ and $$$g(n)$$$ in $$$\mathcal{O}(n)$$$ time.
